BBC Sport Football

How much prize money have Premier League teams earned in Europe?

By BBC Sport's Ask Me Anything team

28 May 2026

Arsenal, Aston Villa and Crystal Palace have all represented the Premier League in European competition this season, and the prize money on offer has been substantial.

Arsenal reached the Champions League final against Paris Saint-Germain, securing at least £100m in prize money along the way. Their run to the final in Munich has been one of the most remarkable in the club's history, coming just days after they sealed the Premier League title for the first time in 22 years.

Aston Villa competed in the Europa League, while Crystal Palace took part in the UEFA Conference League. Both clubs earned significant sums from their respective campaigns.

The UEFA prize money structure rewards clubs at every stage of the competition. Group stage participation alone brings in millions of euros, with bonuses for each win and further payments for advancing through knockout rounds.

Arsenal's Champions League journey has been particularly lucrative. Starting from the group stage, each victory in the knockout rounds added tens of millions of euros to their total. By reaching the final, they have already guaranteed a minimum payout that dwarfs anything they have earned in Europe before.

Aston Villa's Europa League run was also financially rewarding. The Birmingham club navigated through the group stage and into the knockout rounds, earning performance bonuses at each stage. Although they did not reach the final, their European adventure added a significant sum to the club's coffers.

Crystal Palace, competing in the Conference League for the first time, also benefited from UEFA's prize money system. While the sums in the Conference League are smaller than those in the Champions League and Europa League, they still represent meaningful income for a club of Palace's size.

The financial rewards from European football have a knock-on effect on the Premier League as a whole. Money earned in Europe can be reinvested in transfer activity, infrastructure, and youth development, helping clubs to remain competitive both domestically and on the continent.

UEFA's distribution model also includes solidarity payments, which are shared among clubs in member associations even if they did not compete in European competition themselves. This means that all 20 Premier League clubs benefit, to some degree, from the success of those who qualified for Europe.

The exact figures will be confirmed at the end of the season, once all competitions have concluded, but early estimates suggest Arsenal alone could earn in excess of £120m from their Champions League campaign if they win the trophy on Saturday evening in Munich.
